event.text: # Project Management Plan Update —

MAAP-1

**Document Number:** AF-MAAP1-PMP-UPDATE-

001
**Revision:** A
**Date:** January 2025
**Classification:** Program Sensitive
**Change Reference:** Flight Computer Supplier Notification —

12-Month Semiconductor Allocation Shortage, IMA Core Processor
**Contract Type

:** Firm Fixed Price (FFP)
**Program:** Multi-Mission Autonomous

Aircraft Platform (MAAP-1)
**Prepared by:** Program Management

Office, Eurus Systems
**Distribution:** Program Director |
Chief Engineer | VP Supply Chain | CFO | Contracts | Customer Program

Office

---

## 1. Introduction

### 1.1 Purpose of This Update

This Project Management Plan (PMP) Update
documents the programmatic response to a formally notified 12-month semiconductor allocation shortage affecting
IMA (Integrated Modular Avionics) core processor deliveries from the MAAP-1 flight
computer supplier. The update is issued as a controlled revision to the program baseline and supers
edes all prior informal guidance on this subject. It establishes revised
management direction across schedule, cost, risk, procurement, quality, and resource

domains.

The shortage notification constitutes a Severity 1 supply
chain event under the program's event-driven risk escalation protocol. It directly
invalidates two explicit baseline assumptions documented in the Financial and Contract
Management Plan: (1) that Tier 1 suppliers deliver on
time at a rate exceeding 95%, and (2) that no single
-source supplier failures occur. Both assumptions are simultaneously breached by this notification.

### 1.2 Scope of Impact

The IMA core processor is not a peripheral component.
It is the computational substrate of the common Green Aircraft
avionics architecture shared across all three MAAP-1 variants — MAAP-1F (
Wildfire), MAAP-1I (Sentinel), and MAAP-1C (Atlas). As
a configuration-controlled common system under Program Requirements
Baseline Section 5.1, any deviation from the qualified processor baseline requires a waiver co
-signed by the Chief Engineer and the Program Executive Officer, accompanied by a full cost,
schedule, and logistics impact assessment across all three variants. This constraint means the shortage
does not merely affect a single production line — it affects every aircraft built under
this program simultaneously, and every mitigation pathway that

involves hardware substitution carries its own formal governance burden.

The
IMA core processor is classified as a Long Lead (LL) Buy item in
the Bill of Materials, is on the IMS critical path with
zero float through LRIP-3
(aircraft #22), and is the execution platform for the Autonomous Flight Control
System (AFCS), the GNC computers, and the flight-critical software
stack developed to DO-178C DAL A ass
urance level. Loss of this component is not an engineering inconvenience —

it is a program-stopping event at both the production and certification levels.

### 1.3

Authority and Governance

This PMP Update is issued under Program Control Board (PCB) Class
I change authority. It requires Program Manager approval and Milestone
Decision Authority (MDA) notification per Program Requirements Baseline Section 12.2. All mit
igation actions described herein that affect the cost or
schedule baseline are subject to CCB disposition before execution. This document does not itself authorize
expenditure; it establishes the management framework within which authorized expenditures will

be tracked and controlled.

### 1.4 Relationship to Baseline Documents

This update mod
ifies or places holds on the following controlled baseline documents, all of which require formal revision upon

PCB approval of the mitigation strategy:

| Document | Impact | Action Required |
|---|---|---|
| maap1_integrated_master_schedule.md (MAAP1-I
MS-001) | Schedule baseline threatened; critical path re-analysis required | Formal

Schedule Change Request within 30 days |
| maap1_risk_register.md (MAAP1-RMP
-001) | RISK-SC-003 escalated to Active Issue; new
risks to be added | Immediate update; executive

escalation |
| maap1_financial_and_contract_management.md | Baseline assumptions
violated; reserves and LD provisions inadequate | Cost

baseline review within 30 days |
| maap1_manufacturing_ramp_plan.md (MAAP1-MF
G-PLAN-2024-001) | Phase gate criteria threatened

; supplier communication plan insufficient | Revision within 60 days |
| maap1_supply
_chain_procurement_plan.md | CIL status Green for
IMA processors is incorrect; buffer policy inadequate | Immediate CIL escal

ation; plan revision within 45 days |
| maap1_bill_of_materials.md | Multiple
LL Buy items flagged; cost summary requires shortage provision | BOM annotation

within 30 days |
| maap1_customer_demand_forecast.md (MAAP1-CDF-001) | Major
External Event trigger activated; re-baseline required within 45

days | Formal CDF re-baseline |
| maap1_vertical_integration_and_make_buy
_strategy.md | Buffer stock design basis exceeded; dual-source

timing strategy inadequate | Structural revision within 90 days |
| maap1_program_requirements_baseline.md (AF
-MAAP1-PRB-0001) | Section 5.1 waiver process and new SCRMP

section required | PCB action; PRB amendment |

---

## 2. Project

Scope

### 2.1 Unchanged Program Scope

The program scope baseline — delivery
of 18 LRIP aircraft and 165 F
RP aircraft across three variants, achievement of FAA Type Certification
by Program Month 84, and attainment of Full Operational Capability — remains unchanged. This
PMP Update does not propose a scope reduction. All strategic
objectives defined in the Program Requirements Baseline (SO-1 through SO-7) remain in

force as contractual commitments.

### 2.2 Scope of the

Shortage's Technical Reach

The IMA core processor shortage does not affect the airframe,
propulsion, landing gear, or structural subsystems. All composite manufacturing,
wing fabrication, fuselage assembly, dynamic systems integration, and structural
test activities may proceed on their baseline schedules. The shortage is
bounded to the avionics integration thread of the program, but
because that thread is on the critical path for all downstream milestones — ground
testing, first flight, flight qualification, and production readiness — the downstream scope

impact is program-wide.

The following work scope elements are directly
gated by IMA processor availability and are therefore placed on programmatic hold pending

buffer stock quantification and supply recovery planning:

- **D
TA-3 Avionics Integration Article (WBS
4.6.3):** Avionics bay fabrication may
proceed; avionics integration and software integration activities require physical I

MA processor hardware and cannot advance without it.
- **Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) Integration
Laboratory:** The primary mitigation for RISK-T-001 (
AFCS Integration Complexity, currently the highest-rated technical
risk at Score 16) depends on flight-representative processor
hardware being installed in the HIL rig. This mit

igation is now conditionally degraded pending processor availability.
- **Flight Test Article
(FTA-1 through FTA-6) Avionics Integration:** All
six FTAs require production-representative IMA processors for av
ionics installation and flight clearance. FTA build sequence priority is addressed

in Section 3.
- **LRIP Production Aircraft (PA-1 through PA-18
):** All 18 LRIP aircraft require IMA processors as a common Green Aircraft component
. No aircraft can complete systems integration, functional checkout

, or acceptance testing without it.
- **Training Device Development:** To the
extent that Level 6/7 fidelity training simulators share the I
MA processor architecture, training device builds are also affected and may compete with
flight articles for processor allocation. Training device builds should be de

ferred in favor of flight articles pending processor availability confirmation.

### 2.3 Scope Additions

Introduced by This Update

The following scope elements are added to the program baseline as direct consequences of the shortage and its
required mitigation. Each requires PCB authorization and cost

baseline amendment before proceeding:

| Scope Addition | WBS Location | Trigger

|
|---|---|---|
| IMA Processor Buffer Stock Emergency Procurement | New W
BS element under 4.5 | Immediate — 72

-hour authorization target |
| Alternate IMA Processor Source Trade Study | New WBS element under

4.5.1 | 30-day initiation |
| MOSA-1 Compliance Assessment for

Alternate Processor | Systems Engineering | 30-day initiation |
| ICD-003 Compatibility Review for Alternate Processor |

Avionics IPT | 30-day initiation |
| Section 5.1 Waiver Package Preparation (if
substitution pursued) | Program Management / Chief Engineer | 45-day completion target

|
| Semiconductor Supply Chain Tier 2/3 Mapping | Supply Chain | 60

-day completion |
| Supplier Financial and Operational Health Assessment —

Flight Computer Supplier | Supply Chain / Contracts | 30-day completion |
| Alternate IMA Processor DO
-254/DO-178C Qualification (if substitution required) | Avionics IPT /
Certification | Parallel path; 18–30

month timeline |

### 2.4 Variant-Level Scope Equalization

Because
the IMA processor is a common Green Aircraft item, no
variant may be preferentially advantaged over another in processor allocation without
a formal PCB decision and Section 5.1 waiver. If const
rained processor supply necessitates a prioritized build sequence, that sequence must be formally
adjudicated at the PCB level with customer concurrence. A proposed
prioritization framework is offered in Section 3; formal adoption requires

PCB and customer approval.

**Note on I
MS Internal Inconsistency:** The IMS contains a date inconsistency between the First
Flight milestone listed as April 2028 in the milestones table and the FTA-1 delivery date of
16-Dec-29 in the deliverables table. This inconsistency must be formally resolved in the next
scheduled IMS update. The schedule impact analysis above applies proport

ionally regardless of which date is the operative baseline.

### 3.2 Critical Path Re

-Analysis Requirement

The IMS currently operates with **zero float on the critical path**
(IMS Section 12.2). The critical path runs through
: System Architecture → PDR → CDR → FTA-
1 Fabrication and Assembly → First Flight → Flight Test Qualification → Production Transition → IO
C. The avionics integration thread, gated by IMA processor availability, feeds directly into this

path through DTA-3 and FTA-1.

A formal Critical Path Method (CP
M) re-analysis is required within 30 days of this update's approval. The re-analysis must:
- Insert IMA processor delivery as an explicit external constraint

node in the CPM network
- Model three scenarios: best case
(existing buffer stock covers DTA-3; shortage resol
ves in 9 months), base case (6-month effective disruption), and worst case (full
12-month disruption plus potential DO-254 re-qualification requirement

for any alternate processor)
- Publish revised critical path duration and float

analysis at the subsystem level
- Determine which aircraft serial numbers are at
risk and quantify schedule slip in weeks by tail

number

The 10–15% schedule reserve maintained at
key milestones (representing approximately 9–14 months on a 92
-month program) is assessed as insufficient to absorb an unmitigated 12-month shortage event

without formal milestone re-baselining.

### 3.3 Proposed Build Sequence Prioritization Under

Constrained Processor Supply

If buffer stock is insufficient to support all parallel build
activities simultaneously, the following priority allocation sequence is recommended for PC

PCB approval and customer concurrence before implementation.

| Priority Rank | Build Activity | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | HI
L Integration Laboratory (2–3 processor units) | Primary mitigation for RISK-T-001 (Critical);

cannot begin AFCS integration without representative hardware |
| 2 | D
TA-3 Avionics Integration Article (2 units) | Cannot slip without
threatening TRR and First Flight; certification baseline depends on this

article |
| 3 | FTA-1 and FTA-2 (flight sets) | First
two flight test articles drive qualification timeline and First Flight milestone

payment ($125M) |
| 4 | FTA-3 through FTA-6 (flight
sets) | Can absorb limited slip if FTA-1/2

are protected; required for swarm testing |
| 5 | LRIP PA-1 through PA-4
(LRIP-1) | Carry 25%
first-unit schedule buffer; can absorb 2–3 months

of slip before threatening IOC |
| 6 | Training Devices | Defer to protect

flight article allocation; training not on critical certification path |

### 3.4 Immediate

Scheduling Actions (72-Hour Window)

The following scheduling actions are authorized under existing

program management authority and do not require PCB approval:

1. The Program
Scheduler will immediately perform a processor-availability-
constrained CPM run and brief the Program Director within

7 business days.
2. The IMS will not be formally
re-baselined until the supplier's written recovery plan is received
and validated. The current baseline is preserved to maintain contract

ual leverage.
3. All activities dependent on IMA processor physical availability are flagged in
the IMS as externally constrained, with constraint dates set to T

BD pending supplier recovery plan.
4. The Supply Chain critical path float analysis (currently
showing zero float through aircraft #22 and 3-week
float by aircraft #22) will be re-run with shortage assumptions

applied and published to the program leadership team.
5. First Customer Delivery (
MAAP-1F to CAL FIRE, Q
3 2027) is elevated to watch-list status. The Supply Chain Director and
Program Manager will co-brief the customer on this milestone within 5 business days per

the event-driven communication protocol.

## 4. Change Management Plan

### 4.1 Classification and

Authority

The IMA core processor shortage constitutes a **PCB Class I change event
** under Program Requirements Baseline Section 12.2. Class I changes affect cost or schedule baseline and require
Program Manager approval with MDA notification. The following conditions, each independently
sufficient to trigger Class I classification, are all simultaneously present

:

- Confirmed cost baseline impact (buffer stock procurement, exped
ite fees, potential re-qualification costs) materially exceeding

the $1M executive approval threshold
- Confirmed schedule baseline threat
to contractually committed milestones (First Customer Delivery Q3 2027, IOC Q1

2030)
- Potential configuration change to a common Green Aircraft system
(IMA core processor), requiring Chief Engineer and P

EO waiver per PRB Section 5.1
- Potential ICD
-003 revision affecting the core avionics bus / mission system

data interface across all three variants

An emergency PCB session
must be convened within 5 business days of this document's approval. The agenda will include: shortage scope
confirmation, buffer stock authorization, build sequence prioritization, customer notification strategy, and management reserve

drawdown request.

### 4.2 Configuration Control Boundaries

The most important
change control boundary for this event is **PRB Section 5.1** — the common
Green Aircraft configuration. The IMA core processor (specifically the mission computers
hardware element) is explicitly listed as a common configuration item. The following

change governance rules apply:

**Rule 1 — No Informal
Substitutions:** No hardware substitution, bridging solution, or alternate processor may
be procured for installation in a flight article without a
formally approved Section 5.1 waiver and associated CCB change documentation
. Procurement for trade study purposes (non-flight units

) may proceed under separate engineering authorization.

**Rule 2 — ICD
-003 Review Mandatory for Any Substitution:** Any alternate processor candidate
must be evaluated against ICD-003 (core avionics bus / mission system data interface) before procurement commitment. A
compatibility assessment report must be completed within 30 days of any alternate processor being identified as

a candidate.

**Rule 3 — MOSA-1 Compliance Non-Negotiable:** Any bridge or
alternate processor must comply with VITA 65/OpenVPX
or an approved successor standard (MOSA-1). No propri
etary interim solution may be adopted that would place the program in
conflict with MOSA-1 or MOSA-4 (no
single-source proprietary data bus without Government-owned
interface specification). A MOSA compliance certification letter from the

Avionics IPT Lead is required before any alternate processor is added to the qualified parts list.

**Rule 4 — Tri-
Variant Impact Assessment Required:** Per PRB Section 5.3, any ICD-003 change or
Section 5.1 waiver requires a full impact assessment across all three variants (MAAP-1F
, MAAP-1I, MAAP-1C). The Avionics IPT is responsible for completing this assessment; it

cannot be delegated to the flight computer supplier.

**Rule 5 — Autonomy Safety Case
Must Be Preserved:** Any processor substitution that affects the hardware
execution environment for DAL A software (Primary G
NC, Lost-Link Contingency Logic,
Autoland Logic) requires re-execution of the safety case analysis and
coordination with the FAA Certification Office before the change
is approved. The Chief Engineer must sign the safety case re-evaluation. This
is non-negotiable given that PRB Section 2.3 Priority 1 is

Flight Safety and Airworthiness.

### 4.3 Change Control Decision Gates

The following decision
gates are established to govern the program's response to the shortage. Each gate requires documented PCB approval before

proceeding to the next.

| Gate | Decision Point | Decision Authority | Target

Date |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gate 0 | Emergency PCB session convened; shortage formally

registered as Class I event | Program Manager | Day 5 |
| Gate 1 | Buffer

stock quantity and cost established; emergency procurement authorized | Program Manager + CFO | Day 10 |
| Gate 2
| Supplier recovery plan received and evaluated; build sequence prioritization approved | Program Director

+ Customer | Day 30 |
| Gate 3 | Alternate processor trade study complete; substitution vs

. wait decision made | Chief Engineer + PCB | Day 60 |
| Gate 4 | If substit
ution: Section 5.1 waiver package submitted and approved | Chief

Engineer + PEO | Day 90 |
| Gate 5 | IMS re-baseline submitted with revised milestone

dates; customer concurrence obtained | Program Manager + MDA | Day 90 |
| Gate 6
| DO-254/DO-178C qualification path confirmed for any alternate processor |

Certification Manager | Day 90 |

### 4.4 Customer Notification and Contract Actions

**
Notification Timeline:** The event-driven communication protocol requires customer notification within 24 hours of a high-impact
risk elevation. This threshold was crossed upon receipt
of the supplier's formal shortage notification. The customer notification letter
must be issued within 5 business days of this PMP Update's approval. The notification
must document: confirmed scope of shortage, mitigation actions initiated, revised
schedule projections (range, not point estimate

) for affected milestones, and decision points requiring customer input.

**Force Majeure Assessment
:** The Contracts and Legal team will assess within 30 days whether
the semiconductor shortage qualifies as an excusable delay under the contract's
applicable clause (FAR 52.249
-14 or equivalent commercial provision). A semiconductor allocation shortage
that is market-wide and foundry-driven may qualify as a
supply chain force majeure event. If excusable delay status is confirmed:
- Pursue formal schedule relief (LD waiver) worth an

estimated $8M–$12M
- Explore a contract modification for

schedule extension and potential recovery cost reimbursement
- Document
the notification date precisely — most excusable delay cla
uses require notification within 30–60 days of discovery; the clock is running

now

**IMS Re-Baseline Protocol:** The IMS will not be re-baselined until: (a
) the supplier's written recovery plan is received and validated, (b) buffer stock strategy is confirmed, and (c) customer concurrence is obtained on
revised milestone dates. Premature re-baselining relinquishes contractual leverage and removes
pressure on the supplier to recover. The baseline is deliberately preserved until the full mit

igation picture is clear.

### 4.5 Document Update Cascade

Upon PC
B approval of the mitigation strategy, the following documents require formal revision in the order listed.
The CCB is responsible for issuing change notices to each document

custodian:

1. Risk Register (MAAP1-RMP-001) — within

5 days of PCB approval
2. Supply Chain Procurement Plan — within 30 days
3. Integrated Master
Schedule (MAAP1-IMS-001) — within 45 days or upon supplier recovery plan receipt,

whichever is later
4. Customer Demand Forecast (MAAP1-CDF-001) — within 45 days (Major External Event trigger)
5.

Bill of Materials — within 30 days (annotation and status update)
6. Financial and Contract

Management Plan — within 30 days
7. Manufacturing Ramp Plan — within 60 days
8. Program Requirements Baseline (AF-MAAP1-PR
B-0001) — within 90 days (upon PCB approval of Section 5.1 waiver package
if substitution pursued, or new SCRMP ann

ex)
9. Vertical Integration and Make/Buy Strategy — within 90 days (structural revision)

## 5. Cost Management Plan & Cost Baseline

### 5.1 Financial Impact Summary

The baseline program
gross margin is **$398M (9.1
%)** on $4,778M revenue under a Firm
Fixed Price contract. Under FFP terms, every dollar of cost impact and
every week of schedule delay falls entirely on Eurus Systems.
There is no cost-plus mechanism, no undefin
itized contract action, and no automatic recovery from the
customer for a commercial supply chain failure. This

is the financial context within which every mitigation decision must be evaluated.

The
shortage, if unmitigated, is assessed to consume
a material fraction of the program's entire gross margin. The following

scenarios bound the financial exposure:

| Scenario | Direct Cost Impact | Revenue Impact (

LD + Milestone Deferral) | Total Impact | Resulting Gross Margin |
|---|

---|---|---|---|
| **Mitigated** (buffer stock + expedite, 3
-month effective disruption) | +$30M | -$5M | -$35M | ~

8.4% |
| **Partially Mitigated** (6-month effective disruption) | +$60M | -$15M | -$75M |

~7.5% |
| **Unmitigated** (full 12-month disruption) | +$85M–$118M | -$20

.9%** |
| **Compounded** (shortage + other active risks simultaneously) | See
Perfect Storm scenario | See Perfect Storm scenario | Program margin goes negative

| **-1.2%** |

The mid-point unmitigated estimate
of approximately $85M in direct costs alone would exha
ust the program's **$74M management reserve (1.5% of contract
value)** and begin eroding program profit. This is the financial threshold that mand

ates VP-level escalation and corporate engagement.

Driver

The $74M management reserve was sized

igation costs are projected to exceed $50M, the CFO must be brief

ption shifts these inflows right. The 2030 plan

estimated -$10M, could deteriorate to **-$50M to
-$80M** if the shortage is unmitigated and milestone payments slip. This approaches

## 8. Quality Management Plan

### 8.1 Quality

Posture Under Shortage Conditions

The IMA core processor shortage creates specific quality risks that are distinct from those
managed under steady-state program conditions. The Quality Manager is directed to issue updated
surveillance and inspection guidance within 14 days covering the

shortage-specific quality scenarios described in this section.

### 8.2 Supplier Quality

Surveillance — Enhanced Requirements

The Quality Management Plan's requirements for supplier quality agreements
and AS9100D compliance remain in full force
and are not reduced by the shortage. The shortage, in fact, creates elevated quality pressure on
the supplier: suppliers facing delivery commitments with constrained material
sometimes introduce quality shortcuts including acceptance of marginal lots,
relaxation of incoming inspection standards, or component substitution without formal engineering

disposition.

The following enhanced surveillance requirements apply to all IMA processor deliveries during

the shortage period:

- **100% Lot Acceptance Testing:** No sampling plan is acceptable for
flight-critical avionics processors during the shortage period. Every unit must be individually

tested against the qualification baseline before acceptance.
- **Full Traceability Verification:** Date
code, lot number, and qualification test data must be verified for every delivered unit. Any

unit without complete traceability documentation is rejected without exception.
- **Counterfeit Parts Screening:** All processor units proc
ured through non-standard channels (broker markets, spot markets, alternative suppliers) must undergo counterfeit part detection screening per AS6081 and AS5553.
The Security Requirements document identifies supply chain infiltration as a Medium
-risk concern; counterfeit semiconductor insertion is a known threat
vector for this program and is elevated to High during shortage conditions

when broker procurement is active.
- **Process Change Notification:** Any
change to the supplier's manufacturing process, sub-tier source, or component
configuration requires formal supplier deviation/waiver processing through
the program CCB before the modified article can be accepted

for flight use. This is non-negotiable.

### 8.3 First

Article Inspection Requirements

If the processor shortage forces
the supplier to change their manufacturing process, sub-tier, or component configuration to fulfill
delivery commitments, a new First Article Inspection (FAI) per AS9102 is required before the modified
article can be accepted for flight use. The Quality Manager will establish a monitoring protocol to detect
any supplier configuration changes that would trigger this requirement. The FAI requirement
adds 4–12 weeks to the acceptance timeline and must be planned

into the schedule recovery assessment.

### 8.4 Broker and Spot Market Procurement

— Special Handling

If broker or spot market procurement is authorized under
the buffer stock strategy (Section 7), the following quality
controls apply and are mandatory. These controls are non-negotiable for DA

L A flight-critical hardware:

1. Only authorized aerospace component distribut
ors (Arrow, Avnet, TTI) with established anti

-counterfeit programs may be used as primary broker sources.
2. Secondary bro
kers with 100% counterfeit screening capability and full chain

-of-custody documentation may be approved on a case-by-case basis by the Quality Manager.
3. Every broker-
sourced unit must pass 100% incoming inspection including electrical testing
, X-ray inspection, decapsulation sampling, and date code verification before being

admitted to the program parts inventory.
4. Broker-sourced units are quarantined from flight

article installation until the full incoming inspection cycle is complete and documented.
5. Any broker-sourced unit that fails
to provide complete traceability to the original semiconductor manufacturer is rejected and destroyed

. There is no waiver path for this requirement.

### 8.5 Alternate

Processor Qualification Quality Requirements

If an alternate processor is pursued under the Section 5.1 waiver path, the full
DO-254 hardware qualification cycle applies without abbreviation. The Quality Manager and Certification
Manager will jointly define the qualification test plan, which must include:
- Environmental qualification (temperature, humidity, vibration, shock, altitude) per
the applicable avionics qualification standard (RTCA/DO

-160)
- Reliability demonstration testing to validate KSA-01

MTBSA ≥ 25 flight hours threshold
- Electromagnetic compatibility (

EMC) testing per applicable standards
- Software target environment compliance verification per DO-178C (this is the
most time-intensive element, as all DA
L A software running on the new processor must be re-verified in the new target

environment)

A commercial-grade processor cannot be substituted for a DO-254-qualified
flight-critical IMA processor without executing this full qualification cycle. There is no abbreviated

path for DAL A hardware under any circumstance.

### 8.6 Out

-of-Sequence Assembly Quality Considerations

If the build sequence re
ordering recommended in Section 3.3 results in partial aircraft builds
(airframes and structural systems assembled without av

ionics), the following quality requirements apply:

- Open avionics bays on partially built
aircraft are subject to a formal Foreign Object Damage (FOD) prevention program. Avionics bay covers must be installed
and logged out-in-out at every access

.
- All partial builds must be documented with an as-built configuration

record that explicitly identifies the deferred avionics installation.
- Work
manship standards for avionics installation when processors eventually arrive must not
be degraded by time pressure to close the schedule gap.
The Quality Manager will issue explicit workmanship guidance before avionics installation res

umes on any partial-build aircraft.

### 8.7 "Right First

Time" Policy — Reaffirmation

The QMP's "Right First Time" policy is
explicitly reaffirmed in the context of this shortage. Schedule pressure generated by the processor
gap must not be used to justify quality holds being lifted
prematurely, inspection requirements being waived, or rework being accepted without full
disposition. Any quality escape during the shortage period that results
in a rework loop will compound the schedule damage already caused
by the supply disruption. The Quality Manager has standing authority to stop work at any point where
quality requirements are being compromised by schedule pressure, and this authority is reinforced by this

PMP Update.

## 9. Staffing & Resource Management

### 9.1 Workforce Impact Overview

The IMA core processor shortage does not reduce the program
's workforce headcount requirement — the program will need every planned position when
production resumes. However, it creates a significant workforce util
ization challenge during the shortage window. Personnel hired or retained to support av
ionics integration, LRIP build-up, and flight test
article completion will have reduced productive work scope during the period
when IMA processor hardware is unavailable. Managing this workforce util
ization gap is a critical leadership responsibility that directly affects both cost and learning curve

continuity.

### 9.2 Avionics Integration and Systems Engineering Team

The av
ionics integration team supporting DTA-3 (WBS 4.6.3
) and the AFCS IPT are the most directly affected workforce
segments. Without physical IMA processor hardware, avionics bay integration and
software integration activities on DTA-3 cannot proceed. The following workforce management actions

are directed:

**Redeployment to Parallel Work:** The avionics integration workforce
should be redirected during the processor gap to activities that do not require physical

processor hardware:
- Avionics bay structural preparation and wiring harness installation (

can proceed without processors)
- Test procedure development and validation on non-flight test bench

equipment
- DO-178C software development and unit testing (processors are not required for early

software development stages)
- HIL lab infrastructure preparation (racks
, cabling, power, networking — everything except

the flight-representative processors)
- Technical documentation,

qualification test planning, and certification data package preparation

**FP
GA Emulation for Early Software Integration:** The Chief Systems Engineer will assess within
21 days whether FPGA-based processor emulation boards can substitute for production-representative processors in
the early software integration environment, enabling DO-178C qualification testing to begin ahead of hardware
availability. This approach preserves software team productivity and may

recover 2–4 months of schedule upon hardware availability.

**Workforce Retention Priority
:** The avionics integration workforce represents specialized skills with long development times —
DO-178C DAL A software engineers, avionics IPT systems engineers, and hardware
qualification specialists are not fungible with general production labor. Retention of this workforce during
the gap period is a higher priority than cost reduction through lay
offs, because losing trained personnel now would extend the actual disru
ption beyond the semiconductor shortage window. Workforce retention incentives should be considered for key personnel with

skill sets critical to post-shortage recovery.

### 9.3 Flight Test Team

The flight test team supporting
FTA fabrication and the TEMP test program is similarly affected by the processor shortage. F
TA avionics installation cannot be completed without processors; however, significant F

TA build progress is achievable in parallel:
- Airframe fabrication and structural assembly (all

FTAs)
- Propulsion system installation and testing
- Landing gear and hydraulic system integration

- Non-avionics ground systems installation
- Flight test instrumentation installation (
instrumentation packages may use different processor hardware than the production av

ionics system)
- Ground support equipment preparation and calibration
The Manufacturing Director will develop a revised FTA build plan within 30 days that identifies
the maximum production value-add achievable on each F
TA without IMA processors, and sequences this work to maintain

workforce productivity and preserve learning curve progress.

### 9.4 Production Manufacturing Team

The
LRIP production manufacturing team is at risk of forced underutilization if LRIP start
slips due to the shortage. The Manufacturing Ramp Plan's
LRIP-2 phase gate (Q1 2027) requires M
RL-8 achievement and supplier on-time delivery greater than 90% for
3 consecutive months. An avionics supplier in active shortage
will fail this criterion, potentially blocking phase gate approval and preventing the

planned production rate increase.

During the shortage window, the production manufacturing team should be

directed to:
- Complete all non-avionics assembly preparation activities that can be performed without IMA processors
-
Advance facility and tooling readiness activities for LRIP-2

and LRIP-3 rate increases
- Execute training and skills development for personnel who will perform av

ionics installation and systems integration once processors are available
- Support the alternate processor qualification effort with production

tooling and fixture development

### 9.5 Supply Chain Organization Resource Requirements
The shortage significantly increases the workload on the Supply Chain organization. The following resource additions are authorized

immediately:

- **Dedicated Shortage Response Team:** A cross-functional team of 4
–6 personnel is established within the Supply Chain organization reporting directly to the Supply Chain Director
. This team is responsible for daily monitoring of processor allocation status, supplier engagement, broker
market surveillance, and buffer stock tracking. This team has no other responsibilities during the active shortage period.
- **Resident Quality Representative:** One Quality Engineer is assigned as resident representative
at the flight computer supplier facility within 30 days. Travel and accommodation costs are charged to the shortage

response cost account.
- **Legal/Contracts Support:** Additional
Contracts and Legal resource is allocated for force majeure assessment, supplier
LD notification, and potential contract modification negotiation.
If internal capacity is insufficient, outside counsel authorization is requested

from the General Counsel within 30 days.

### 9.6 Learning Curve Management —

Critical Human Factors Dimension

The 85% learning curve assumption und
erpinning the FFP pricing is critically dependent on production continu
ity. A 12-month production stoppage does not merely pause learning
— it actively degrades it. Personnel who performed avionics integration 12 months ago and then were
redeployed to other activities will not return to their previous performance level immediately
. The effective learning curve upon restart is likely to be 88%–90
% rather than 85%, generating $40M–$60M in additional labor cost

growth.

To mitigate learning curve regression:
- Workforce redeployment during the gap
must, wherever possible, involve work in the same skill
domain (avionics, systems integration) rather than unrelated activities
- Structured refresher training for avionics integration personnel will be planned and budgeted before production

resumes
- The first LRIP aircraft processed through avionics integration after
the shortage resolves should be treated as a de facto re-first-article
from a quality and productivity monitoring standpoint,

with enhanced supervision and first-unit review cycles

### 9.7 Key

Personnel Dependency Management

The shortage concentrates risk on the few individuals
with deep expertise in IMA processor architecture, DO-178C target environment management
, and AFCS integration. These individuals will be under exceptional demand during the alternate processor assessment
, MOSA compliance verification, and (if required) DO
-254 re-qualification efforts. The Human Resources function will assess the key personnel dependency landscape within
30 days and recommend retention measures for individuals whose departure during the shortage window

would materially impair recovery.

---

## 10. Overall Assessment & Recommended

Path Forward

### 10.1 Executive Summary of Program Exposure

The 12-month IMA core processor semiconductor
allocation shortage is the most significant single supply chain event the MAAP-
1 program has encountered. It is not, at its core, a supply chain problem with
a supply chain solution — it is a **program-level strategic event** that simultaneously threatens the financial
baseline, the schedule baseline, the configuration baseline, the certification
baseline, and the customer relationship. It demands a program-level response led by the Program
Director with corporate visibility, not a supply chain corrective action plan managed

below the VP level.

The exposure is quantified and bounded.
The unmitigated cost impact of $85M–$118M would
exhaust the $74M management reserve entirely and materially impair the
9.1% gross margin on a Firm
Fixed Price contract with no recovery mechanism from the customer for commercial
supply chain events. The schedule impact, if unmitigated, is
a 12-month cascade from DTA-3 through First Flight, LRIP start
, and IOC — a program-level milestone breach with contractual consequences including
liquidated damages at $150K per week and
potential FRP lot option reduction by the

customer.

The good news is that the exposure is manageable if action is taken within the 72-hour window established
by this PMP Update. The mitigation economics are unambiguous: spending $25
M–$40M now avoids $85M–$118M in disruption costs. Every
week of delay in executing the immediate actions described in this document represents additional

schedule consumed and additional cost exposure crystallized.

### 10.2 Five Non-Negotiable Immediate

Decisions

The following five decisions must be made within 72 hours of this document's approval
. They are not recommendations — they are the minimum necessary actions
to prevent the program from entering an unrecoverable posture. Each is

assigned to a named decision authority.

**Decision 1 — Emergency

Buffer Stock Authorization**
*Decision Authority: Program Director + CFO*
*Action:
* Authorize emergency procurement of up to 30–40 IMA core processor units from all available sources,
at a budget authority of up to $25M drawn from management
reserve. Procurement to begin immediately upon authorization. Counterfeit screening
(AS6081) mandatory for all non-standard source units.
*Consequence of inaction:* Every day without authorization is a day the buffer
stock window narrows. Processor allocation availability in shortage conditions
is first-come, first-served. Delay directly reduces the achievable buffer

quantity.

**Decision 2 — Supplier Executive Escalation and Formal Recovery

Plan Demand**
*Decision Authority: VP Supply Chain*
*Action:* Issue formal supplier notification
to the flight computer supplier within 5 business days invoking contractual reporting obligations
, demanding a written recovery plan within 15 business days, and formally documenting the notification date for potential

force majeure and LD purposes.
*Consequence of inaction:* Without a formal demand, the supplier has
no contractual urgency to provide a recovery plan. The program loses contractual leverage
and the force majeure notification clock continues to run against Eurus Systems

.

**Decision 3 — Customer Notification**
*Decision Authority: Program Manager*
*Action:* Issue formal customer notification within 5
business days per the event-driven communication protocol. Notification must cover:
confirmed shortage scope, mitigation actions initiated, schedule risk range
(not point estimate) for affected milestones, and decision
points requiring customer input. Do not wait until the full picture
is clear — the notification obligation runs from the date of discovery

, not the date of certainty.
*Consequence of inaction:* Late customer notification destroys the trust relationship needed
to negotiate schedule relief, LD waivers, and potential
contract modifications. It also eliminates the force majeure notification window if one is applicable

.

**Decision 4 — Emergency PCB Session**
*Decision Authority: Program Manager*
*Action:*
Convene an emergency PCB session within 5 business days to formally register the shortage as a Class
I event, approve the build sequence prioritization framework, authorize the management reserve draw
down, and assign ownership of all Tier 1 mitigation actions.
*Consequence of inaction:* Without formal PCB registration, mitigation actions will be executed
outside the change control framework, creating configuration audit
findings, untracked cost growth, and potential MDA

visibility gaps.

**Decision 5 — Alternate Processor Trade Study Initiation**
*Decision Authority: Chief Engineer*
*Action:*
Task the Avionics IPT to initiate a formal alternate processor trade study within 30
days, assessing: MOSA-1 compliance, ICD-003 compatibility, KSA-01
reliability implications, and DO-254/DO-178C re-qualification scope. The
trade study does not commit the program to a substitution — it creates the

informed basis for the Gate 3 decision at Day 60.
*Consequence of inaction:* If
the trade study is not begun now, the Gate 3 decision at Day 60 will have no factual basis, and the program will be forced
to either wait passively for the shortage to resolve or make an uninformed substit

ution decision. Neither is acceptable.

### 10.3 Decision Hierarchy and Trade-

Space Adjudication

Multiple competing pressures will operate simultaneously during
the shortage response: pressure to keep the production line moving, pressure to minimize
cost premium for buffer stock, pressure to resolve the shortage quickly with a hardware
substitution, and pressure to maintain the certification baseline. The following priority hierarchy, derived
directly from Program Requirements Baseline Section 2.3, gov

erns all trade-space adjudication:

**Priority 1 — Flight Safety and Airw
orthiness:** No action that compromises the autonomy safety
case, the DO-178C/DO-254 qualification baseline, or the FA
A certification data package is acceptable regardless of schedule or cost pressure.
A non-qualified processor is not an acceptable bridge solution.
A qualification shortcut is not acceptable regardless of supplier ass

urances.

**Priority 2 — Mission Performance KPPs:** K
PP-06 (Net-Ready) and KPP-07 (Autonomous
Operation) are JROC-controlled requirements. Any
hardware substitution that cannot be demonstrated to maintain these KPPs at
or above their threshold values is not acceptable without MDA concurrence.
**Priority 3 — Schedule:** Schedule recovery is a legitimate program
objective, but it is explicitly subordinate to Safety and KPP maintenance. Schedule pressure

must not drive quality, safety, or certification compromises.

**Priority 4 — Cost
:** Cost efficiency is important on a margin-stressed FFP program, but the hierarchy
makes clear that spending appropriately on buffer stock, alternate qualification, and supplier
escalation is the correct decision even if it is expensive. The alternative —
a non-airworthy aircraft or a missed certification milestone — is far

more costly.

### 10.4 Strategic Path Forward

The program's response to this
shortage will define its resilience posture for the remainder of the production

run. The following strategic path is recommended:

**Phase 1 (Days 0

–30): Stabilize**
Protect the development and early flight
test build schedule through emergency buffer stock procurement. Prevent the shortage
from consuming the DTA-3 and FTA-1/2
schedule. Establish formal governance through PCB Class I process
. Notify the customer and preserve force majeure rights. Do

not re-baseline the IMS. Do not make premature substitution decisions.

**

Phase 2 (Days 30–90): Assess and Decide**
Receive and validate the supplier's
recovery plan. Complete the alternate processor trade study. Make the Gate 3 decision: wait for primary processor recovery with buffer
stock protection, or pursue a qualified alternate. If alternate is pursued, init
iate the Section 5.1 waiver process and DO-254/DO-178C qualification path immediately
. Conduct the IMS CPM re-analysis and
prepare revised milestone dates for customer negotiation.

Assess force majeure applicability and initiate contract modification discussions.

**Phase 3

(Days 90–365): Recover and Harden**
Execute the chosen mitigation path — buffer
stock protection or alternate processor qualification — with full governance discipline. Negotiate customer
concurrence on revised milestone dates. Draw down management reserve in a controlled and
documented manner. Accelerate the dual-source qualification effort to LRIP-1 completion. Negotiate long-term found
ry allocation agreements covering FRP quantities. Revise the supply chain resilience framework to address
market-wide semiconductor allocation shortages — a risk category

that was not previously modeled in the program's risk management architecture.

**Phase 4 (Post-Recovery

): Prevent Recurrence**
Upon shortage resolution, conduct a formal lessons-learned review. Rev
ise the Program Requirements Baseline to include a Supply Chain Risk Management Plan
annex establishing LLI registry maintenance, minimum
buffer stock calculation methodology, and sole-source risk mitigation requirements for all
common Green Aircraft configuration items. Update the Vertical
Integration and Make/Buy Strategy to increase buffer stock design basis from 6
months to 12–18 months for semiconductor-dependent avionics components. Institut
ionalize Tier 2/3 supply chain visibility for all flight-critical processor components.
These changes must be in place before FRP lot planning

begins.

### 10.5 The One Overriding Principle

Every decision made during
this shortage response must be traceable to a single principle: **the program must deliver air
worthy aircraft that meet their KPPs on a schedule that can be defended
to the customer, the regulator, and the Milestone Decision Authority.
** No shortcut that compromises airworthiness is recoverable. No informal
decision that bypasses the PCB and CCB process is auditable. No buffer
stock procurement that skips counterfeit screening is acceptable for DAL A hardware. The
shortage is a serious but survivable event. A compromised aircraft or

a failed certification audit is not.

Act now. Act in order of
priority. Act within the governance framework. The program has the tools,
the reserves, and the architectural flexibility — through the MOSA open
architecture, the multi-vendor OpenVPX ecosystem, and the documented dual
-source qualification path — to emerge from this shortage with its technical baseline and customer relationships
intact. The window for decisive action is open today. It will not

remain open indefinitely.

---

| Revision

| Date | Author | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | January 2025 | Program Management Office
| Initial issue — IMA Core Processor Shortage Response |

**Approvals Required:**

| Role | Signature | Date |

| Program Manager | | |

| VP

Supply Chain | | |
| CFO | | |
| Contracts Manager | | |

Program Director | Chief Engineer | VP Supply Chain | CFO | Contracts Manager | Risk Manager
| Quality Manager | Manufacturing Director | Customer Program Office (upon
PCB approval)

# Project Management Plan Update — MAAP-1

**Document Number:** AF-MAAP1-PMP-UPDATE-001
**Revision:** A
**Date:** January 2025
**Classification:** Program Sensitive
**Change Reference:** Flight Computer Supplier Notification — 12-Month Semiconductor Allocation Shortage, IMA Core Processor
**Contract Type:** Firm Fixed Price (FFP)
**Program:** Multi-Mission Autonomous Aircraft Platform (MAAP-1)
**Prepared by:** Program Management Office, Eurus Systems
**Distribution:** Program Director | Chief Engineer | VP Supply Chain | CFO | Contracts | Customer Program Office

---

## 1. Introduction

### 1.1 Purpose of This Update

This Project Management Plan (PMP) Update documents the programmatic response to a formally notified 12-month semiconductor allocation shortage affecting IMA (Integrated Modular Avionics) core processor deliveries from the MAAP-1 flight computer supplier. The update is issued as a controlled revision to the program baseline and supersedes all prior informal guidance on this subject. It establishes revised management direction across schedule, cost, risk, procurement, quality, and resource domains.

The shortage notification constitutes a Severity 1 supply chain event under the program's event-driven risk escalation protocol. It directly invalidates two explicit baseline assumptions documented in the Financial and Contract Management Plan: (1) that Tier 1 suppliers deliver on time at a rate exceeding 95%, and (2) that no single-source supplier failures occur. Both assumptions are simultaneously breached by this notification.

### 1.2 Scope of Impact

The IMA core processor is not a peripheral component. It is the computational substrate of the common Green Aircraft avionics architecture shared across all three MAAP-1 variants — MAAP-1F (Wildfire), MAAP-1I (Sentinel), and MAAP-1C (Atlas). As a configuration-controlled common system under Program Requirements Baseline Section 5.1, any deviation from the qualified processor baseline requires a waiver co-signed by the Chief Engineer and the Program Executive Officer, accompanied by a full cost, schedule, and logistics impact assessment across all three variants. This constraint means the shortage does not merely affect a single production line — it affects every aircraft built under this program simultaneously, and every mitigation pathway that involves hardware substitution carries its own formal governance burden.

The IMA core processor is classified as a Long Lead (LL) Buy item in the Bill of Materials, is on the IMS critical path with zero float through LRIP-3 (aircraft #22), and is the execution platform for the Autonomous Flight Control System (AFCS), the GNC computers, and the flight-critical software stack developed to DO-178C DAL A assurance level. Loss of this component is not an engineering inconvenience — it is a program-stopping event at both the production and certification levels.

### 1.3 Authority and Governance

This PMP Update is issued under Program Control Board (PCB) Class I change authority. It requires Program Manager approval and Milestone Decision Authority (MDA) notification per Program Requirements Baseline Section 12.2. All mitigation actions described herein that affect the cost or schedule baseline are subject to CCB disposition before execution. This document does not itself authorize expenditure; it establishes the management framework within which authorized expenditures will be tracked and controlled.

### 1.4 Relationship to Baseline Documents

This update modifies or places holds on the following controlled baseline documents, all of which require formal revision upon PCB approval of the mitigation strategy:

| Document | Impact | Action Required |
|---|---|---|
| maap1_integrated_master_schedule.md (MAAP1-IMS-001) | Schedule baseline threatened; critical path re-analysis required | Formal Schedule Change Request within 30 days |
| maap1_risk_register.md (MAAP1-RMP-001) | RISK-SC-003 escalated to Active Issue; new risks to be added | Immediate update; executive escalation |
| maap1_financial_and_contract_management.md | Baseline assumptions violated; reserves and LD provisions inadequate | Cost baseline review within 30 days |
| maap1_manufacturing_ramp_plan.md (MAAP1-MFG-PLAN-2024-001) | Phase gate criteria threatened; supplier communication plan insufficient | Revision within 60 days |
| maap1_supply_chain_procurement_plan.md | CIL status Green for IMA processors is incorrect; buffer policy inadequate | Immediate CIL escalation; plan revision within 45 days |
| maap1_bill_of_materials.md | Multiple LL Buy items flagged; cost summary requires shortage provision | BOM annotation within 30 days |
| maap1_customer_demand_forecast.md (MAAP1-CDF-001) | Major External Event trigger activated; re-baseline required within 45 days | Formal CDF re-baseline |
| maap1_vertical_integration_and_make_buy_strategy.md | Buffer stock design basis exceeded; dual-source timing strategy inadequate | Structural revision within 90 days |
| maap1_program_requirements_baseline.md (AF-MAAP1-PRB-0001) | Section 5.1 waiver process and new SCRMP section required | PCB action; PRB amendment |

---

## 2. Project Scope

### 2.1 Unchanged Program Scope

The program scope baseline — delivery of 18 LRIP aircraft and 165 FRP aircraft across three variants, achievement of FAA Type Certification by Program Month 84, and attainment of Full Operational Capability — remains unchanged. This PMP Update does not propose a scope reduction. All strategic objectives defined in the Program Requirements Baseline (SO-1 through SO-7) remain in force as contractual commitments.

### 2.2 Scope of the Shortage's Technical Reach

The IMA core processor shortage does not affect the airframe, propulsion, landing gear, or structural subsystems. All composite manufacturing, wing fabrication, fuselage assembly, dynamic systems integration, and structural test activities may proceed on their baseline schedules. The shortage is bounded to the avionics integration thread of the program, but because that thread is on the critical path for all downstream milestones — ground testing, first flight, flight qualification, and production readiness — the downstream scope impact is program-wide.

The following work scope elements are directly gated by IMA processor availability and are therefore placed on programmatic hold pending buffer stock quantification and supply recovery planning:

- **DTA-3 Avionics Integration Article (WBS 4.6.3):** Avionics bay fabrication may proceed; avionics integration and software integration activities require physical IMA processor hardware and cannot advance without it.
- **Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) Integration Laboratory:** The primary mitigation for RISK-T-001 (AFCS Integration Complexity, currently the highest-rated technical risk at Score 16) depends on flight-representative processor hardware being installed in the HIL rig. This mitigation is now conditionally degraded pending processor availability.
- **Flight Test Article (FTA-1 through FTA-6) Avionics Integration:** All six FTAs require production-representative IMA processors for avionics installation and flight clearance. FTA build sequence priority is addressed in Section 3.
- **LRIP Production Aircraft (PA-1 through PA-18):** All 18 LRIP aircraft require IMA processors as a common Green Aircraft component. No aircraft can complete systems integration, functional checkout, or acceptance testing without it.
- **Training Device Development:** To the extent that Level 6/7 fidelity training simulators share the IMA processor architecture, training device builds are also affected and may compete with flight articles for processor allocation. Training device builds should be deferred in favor of flight articles pending processor availability confirmation.

### 2.3 Scope Additions Introduced by This Update

The following scope elements are added to the program baseline as direct consequences of the shortage and its required mitigation. Each requires PCB authorization and cost baseline amendment before proceeding:

| Scope Addition | WBS Location | Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| IMA Processor Buffer Stock Emergency Procurement | New WBS element under 4.5 | Immediate — 72-hour authorization target |
| Alternate IMA Processor Source Trade Study | New WBS element under 4.5.1 | 30-day initiation |
| MOSA-1 Compliance Assessment for Alternate Processor | Systems Engineering | 30-day initiation |
| ICD-003 Compatibility Review for Alternate Processor | Avionics IPT | 30-day initiation |
| Section 5.1 Waiver Package Preparation (if substitution pursued) | Program Management / Chief Engineer | 45-day completion target |
| Semiconductor Supply Chain Tier 2/3 Mapping | Supply Chain | 60-day completion |
| Supplier Financial and Operational Health Assessment — Flight Computer Supplier | Supply Chain / Contracts | 30-day completion |
| Alternate IMA Processor DO-254/DO-178C Qualification (if substitution required) | Avionics IPT / Certification | Parallel path; 18–30 month timeline |

### 2.4 Variant-Level Scope Equalization

Because the IMA processor is a common Green Aircraft item, no variant may be preferentially advantaged over another in processor allocation without a formal PCB decision and Section 5.1 waiver. If constrained processor supply necessitates a prioritized build sequence, that sequence must be formally adjudicated at the PCB level with customer concurrence. A proposed prioritization framework is offered in Section 3; formal adoption requires PCB and customer approval.

---

## 3. Milestone List & Schedule Baseline

### 3.1 Current Baseline Milestones and Status

The table below reflects the January 2025 IMS baseline dates and the current assessed status of each major milestone in light of the IMA processor shortage notification. Status reflects the unmitigated worst case unless buffer stock or alternative sourcing actions are confirmed. No formal IMS re-baseline has been executed; this assessment is provided to bound the risk and support decision-making.

| Milestone | Baseline Date | Shortage Impact (Unmitigated) | Shortage Impact (Partially Mitigated) | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Long-Lead Item Procurement Complete (WBS 4.5.2) | 23-Oct-28 | Extends to mid-to-late 2029 | Extends 3–6 months | 🔴 AT RISK |
| DTA-3 Avionics Integration Complete | ~25-Sep-28 | Slips ~12 months to Sep-29 | Slips 3–5 months | 🔴 AT RISK |
| DTA-3 Software Integration Complete | ~21-Nov-28 | Slips ~12 months to Nov-29 | Slips 3–5 months | 🔴 AT RISK |
| Test Readiness Review (TRR) | May-28 | Slips 6–10 months | Slips 2–4 months | 🔴 AT RISK |
| First Flight | Q2 2028 | Slips 6–12 months | Slips 2–4 months | 🔴 AT RISK |
| First Customer Delivery (Q3 2027) | Q3 2027 | At direct risk | Marginal — depends on buffer stock | 🔴 AT RISK |
| FTA-1 Delivery | 16-Dec-29 | Slips to ~Dec-30 | Slips 2–4 months | 🔴 AT RISK |
| Flight Qualification Complete | Oct-29 | Slips 4–8 months | Slips 1–3 months | 🟠 HIGH RISK |
| Production Readiness Review (PRR) | Nov-29 | Slips 4–8 months | Slips 1–3 months | 🟠 HIGH RISK |
| LRIP Start | Jan-30 | Slips 4–8 months | Slips 1–3 months | 🟠 HIGH RISK |
| IOC (Wildfire — MAAP-1F) | Q1 2030 | Slips to Q1–Q3 2031 | Slips 1–2 months | 🟠 HIGH RISK |
| IOC (Military — MAAP-1I) | Q4 2030 | Slips 3–6 months | Slips 1–2 months | 🟡 ELEVATED |
| FRP Decision Gate | Jul-30 | Slips 3–6 months | Slips 1–2 months | 🟡 ELEVATED |
| FRP Start (18 aircraft/year) | PM 108 | At risk if LRIP performance disrupted | Monitor | 🟡 ELEVATED |
| FAA Type Certificate | PM 84 | At risk if flight test program compressed | Monitor | 🟡 ELEVATED |

**Note on IMS Internal Inconsistency:** The IMS contains a date inconsistency between the First Flight milestone listed as April 2028 in the milestones table and the FTA-1 delivery date of 16-Dec-29 in the deliverables table. This inconsistency must be formally resolved in the next scheduled IMS update. The schedule impact analysis above applies proportionally regardless of which date is the operative baseline.

### 3.2 Critical Path Re-Analysis Requirement

The IMS currently operates with **zero float on the critical path** (IMS Section 12.2). The critical path runs through: System Architecture → PDR → CDR → FTA-1 Fabrication and Assembly → First Flight → Flight Test Qualification → Production Transition → IOC. The avionics integration thread, gated by IMA processor availability, feeds directly into this path through DTA-3 and FTA-1.

A formal Critical Path Method (CPM) re-analysis is required within 30 days of this update's approval. The re-analysis must:
- Insert IMA processor delivery as an explicit external constraint node in the CPM network
- Model three scenarios: best case (existing buffer stock covers DTA-3; shortage resolves in 9 months), base case (6-month effective disruption), and worst case (full 12-month disruption plus potential DO-254 re-qualification requirement for any alternate processor)
- Publish revised critical path duration and float analysis at the subsystem level
- Determine which aircraft serial numbers are at risk and quantify schedule slip in weeks by tail number

The 10–15% schedule reserve maintained at key milestones (representing approximately 9–14 months on a 92-month program) is assessed as insufficient to absorb an unmitigated 12-month shortage event without formal milestone re-baselining.

### 3.3 Proposed Build Sequence Prioritization Under Constrained Processor Supply

If buffer stock is insufficient to support all parallel build activities simultaneously, the following priority allocation sequence is recommended for PCB adjudication. This sequence is proposed, not authorized; it requires PCB approval and customer concurrence before implementation.

| Priority Rank | Build Activity | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | HIL Integration Laboratory (2–3 processor units) | Primary mitigation for RISK-T-001 (Critical); cannot begin AFCS integration without representative hardware |
| 2 | DTA-3 Avionics Integration Article (2 units) | Cannot slip without threatening TRR and First Flight; certification baseline depends on this article |
| 3 | FTA-1 and FTA-2 (flight sets) | First two flight test articles drive qualification timeline and First Flight milestone payment ($125M) |
| 4 | FTA-3 through FTA-6 (flight sets) | Can absorb limited slip if FTA-1/2 are protected; required for swarm testing |
| 5 | LRIP PA-1 through PA-4 (LRIP-1) | Carry 25% first-unit schedule buffer; can absorb 2–3 months of slip before threatening IOC |
| 6 | Training Devices | Defer to protect flight article allocation; training not on critical certification path |

### 3.4 Immediate Scheduling Actions (72-Hour Window)

The following scheduling actions are authorized under existing program management authority and do not require PCB approval:

1. The Program Scheduler will immediately perform a processor-availability-constrained CPM run and brief the Program Director within 7 business days.
2. The IMS will not be formally re-baselined until the supplier's written recovery plan is received and validated. The current baseline is preserved to maintain contractual leverage.
3. All activities dependent on IMA processor physical availability are flagged in the IMS as externally constrained, with constraint dates set to TBD pending supplier recovery plan.
4. The Supply Chain critical path float analysis (currently showing zero float through aircraft #22 and 3-week float by aircraft #22) will be re-run with shortage assumptions applied and published to the program leadership team.
5. First Customer Delivery (MAAP-1F to CAL FIRE, Q3 2027) is elevated to watch-list status. The Supply Chain Director and Program Manager will co-brief the customer on this milestone within 5 business days per the event-driven communication protocol.

---

## 4. Change Management Plan

### 4.1 Classification and Authority

The IMA core processor shortage constitutes a **PCB Class I change event** under Program Requirements Baseline Section 12.2. Class I changes affect cost or schedule baseline and require Program Manager approval with MDA notification. The following conditions, each independently sufficient to trigger Class I classification, are all simultaneously present:

- Confirmed cost baseline impact (buffer stock procurement, expedite fees, potential re-qualification costs) materially exceeding the $1M executive approval threshold
- Confirmed schedule baseline threat to contractually committed milestones (First Customer Delivery Q3 2027, IOC Q1 2030)
- Potential configuration change to a common Green Aircraft system (IMA core processor), requiring Chief Engineer and PEO waiver per PRB Section 5.1
- Potential ICD-003 revision affecting the core avionics bus / mission system data interface across all three variants

An emergency PCB session must be convened within 5 business days of this document's approval. The agenda will include: shortage scope confirmation, buffer stock authorization, build sequence prioritization, customer notification strategy, and management reserve drawdown request.

### 4.2 Configuration Control Boundaries

The most important change control boundary for this event is **PRB Section 5.1** — the common Green Aircraft configuration. The IMA core processor (specifically the mission computers hardware element) is explicitly listed as a common configuration item. The following change governance rules apply:

**Rule 1 — No Informal Substitutions:** No hardware substitution, bridging solution, or alternate processor may be procured for installation in a flight article without a formally approved Section 5.1 waiver and associated CCB change documentation. Procurement for trade study purposes (non-flight units) may proceed under separate engineering authorization.

**Rule 2 — ICD-003 Review Mandatory for Any Substitution:** Any alternate processor candidate must be evaluated against ICD-003 (core avionics bus / mission system data interface) before procurement commitment. A compatibility assessment report must be completed within 30 days of any alternate processor being identified as a candidate.

**Rule 3 — MOSA-1 Compliance Non-Negotiable:** Any bridge or alternate processor must comply with VITA 65/OpenVPX or an approved successor standard (MOSA-1). No proprietary interim solution may be adopted that would place the program in conflict with MOSA-1 or MOSA-4 (no single-source proprietary data bus without Government-owned interface specification). A MOSA compliance certification letter from the Avionics IPT Lead is required before any alternate processor is added to the qualified parts list.

**Rule 4 — Tri-Variant Impact Assessment Required:** Per PRB Section 5.3, any ICD-003 change or Section 5.1 waiver requires a full impact assessment across all three variants (MAAP-1F, MAAP-1I, MAAP-1C). The Avionics IPT is responsible for completing this assessment; it cannot be delegated to the flight computer supplier.

**Rule 5 — Autonomy Safety Case Must Be Preserved:** Any processor substitution that affects the hardware execution environment for DAL A software (Primary GNC, Lost-Link Contingency Logic, Autoland Logic) requires re-execution of the safety case analysis and coordination with the FAA Certification Office before the change is approved. The Chief Engineer must sign the safety case re-evaluation. This is non-negotiable given that PRB Section 2.3 Priority 1 is Flight Safety and Airworthiness.

The notification must document: confirmed scope of shortage, mitigation actions initiated, revised schedule projections (range, not point estimate) for affected milestones, and decision points requiring customer input.

**Force Majeure Assessment:** The Contracts and Legal team will assess within 30 days whether the semiconductor shortage qualifies as an excusable delay under the contract's applicable clause (FAR 52.249-14 or equivalent commercial provision). - Document the notification date precisely — most excusable delay clauses require notification within 30–60 days of discovery; the clock is running now

**IMS Re-Baseline Protocol:** The IMS will not be re-baselined until: (a) the supplier's written recovery plan is received and validated, (b) buffer stock strategy is confirmed, and (c) customer concurrence is obtained on revised milestone dates. Premature re-baselining relinquishes contractual leverage and removes pressure on the supplier to recover. The baseline is deliberately preserved until the full mitigation picture is clear.

## 5. Cost Management Plan & Cost Baseline

### 5.1 Financial Impact Summary

The baseline program gross margin is **$398M (9.1%)** on $4,778M revenue under a Firm Fixed Price contract. Under FFP terms, every dollar of cost impact and every week of schedule delay falls entirely on Eurus Systems. There is no cost-plus mechanism, no undefinitized contract action, and no automatic recovery from the customer for a commercial supply chain failure. This is the financial context within which every mitigation decision must be evaluated.

The shortage, if unmitigated, is assessed to consume a material fraction of the program's entire gross margin. The following scenarios bound the financial exposure:

| Scenario | Direct Cost Impact | Revenue Impact (LD + Milestone Deferral) | Total Impact | Resulting Gross Margin |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mitigated** (buffer stock + expedite, 3-month effective disruption) | +$30M | -$5M | -$35M | ~8.4% |
| **Partially Mitigated** (6-month effective disruption) | +$60M | -$15M | -$75M | ~7.5% |
| **Unmitigated** (full 12-month disruption) | +$85M–$118M | -$20M–$35M | -$105M–$153M | **6.1%–6.9%** |
| **Compounded** (shortage + other active risks simultaneously) | See Perfect Storm scenario | See Perfect Storm scenario | Program margin goes negative | **-1.2%** |

The mid-point unmitigated estimate of approximately $85M in direct costs alone would exhaust the program's **$74M management reserve (1.5% of contract value)** and begin eroding program profit. This is the financial threshold that mandates VP-level escalation and corporate engagement.

### 5.2 Cost Impact by Driver

The following cost elements are quantified from the multi-domain analysis. These are estimates for planning purposes; a formal Estimate at Completion (EAC) revision will be produced within 30 days.

### 5.4 Management Reserve Adequacy Assessment

The $74M management reserve was sized against the existing risk register, which did not include a confirmed 12-month IMA processor shortage as a discrete Active Issue. Competing demands on this reserve must now be re-evaluated:

- The reserve was designed to absorb the existing documented risk portfolio probabilistically. Adding a confirmed $85M mid-point exposure without additional reserve authorization creates a structural deficit.
- The $74M reserve is insufficient to cover even the mid-point unmitigated scenario without Program reserve exhaustion.
- Formal management reserve drawdown authorization is required from the Program Director immediately for buffer stock procurement (estimated $15M–$25M).
- If mitigation costs are projected to exceed $50M, the CFO must be briefed and a corporate contingency reserve request initiated. The Financial and Contract Management Plan identifies $150M corporate contingency availability.

### 5.5 Cash Flow Implications

The program's cumulative cash position is already negative through 2031 under the baseline plan, with positive cumulative cash not achieved until 2032. The shortage creates three compounding cash problems that worsen this already-strained profile:

**Problem 1 — Milestone Payment Deferral:** IOC milestone ($35M) and LRIP delivery payments are triggered by aircraft acceptance. A 6–12 month production disruption shifts these inflows right. The 2030 plan shows $685M inflow; a 10% delivery shortfall represents a $68M cash shortfall in that year alone.

**Problem 2 — Cost Outflows Continue:** Labor, overhead, and facility costs at approximately $52–57M per month during the LRIP/FRP ramp continue regardless of production rate. Net cash burn accelerates during a production stoppage.

**Problem 3 — Buffer Stock Working Capital:** Emergency buffer stock procurement requires $15M–$25M in immediate capital deployment before the shortage fully materializes. The program must fund this from internal cash or request corporate support within the 72-hour decision window.

The cumulative cash position, already negative through 2031 at an estimated -$10M, could deteriorate to **-$50M to -$80M** if the shortage is unmitigated and milestone payments slip. This approaches the threshold where corporate reserve funding becomes necessary.

### 5.6 Mitigation Economics — Buffer Stock ROI

The mitigation economics are unambiguous. Spending $25M–$40M now to secure buffer stock and initiate alternate sourcing avoids an estimated $85M–$118M in disruption costs. This represents a return on mitigation investment of approximately 2.1x to 4.7x. The decision to authorize emergency buffer stock procurement is the single highest-ROI action available to the program and must be made within 72 hours of this document's approval.

### 5.7 Liquidated Damages Reserve Re-Baselining

The existing $12M LD reserve was calibrated at 15% probability of occurrence. A confirmed 12-month supplier shortage materially increases both the probability and magnitude of LD exposure:

- LD risk probability is re-assessed at **40–60%** given the confirmed shortage
- Expected LD exposure recalculates to **$20M–$32M** under the revised probability
- The existing $12M LD reserve is **structurally deficient** by $8M–$20M
- CFO authorization is required to augment the LD reserve to a minimum of **$25M** within 30 days

### 5.8 Cost Control Actions

The following cost control actions are established for immediate implementation:

1. **72-Hour Authorization:** Program Director and CFO authorize emergency buffer stock procurement up to $25M from management reserve. No further CCB action required for this specific expenditure up to this limit.
2. **30-Day Action:** Program Controller conducts off-cycle management reserve adequacy assessment against the revised risk profile. Results briefed to VP-level leadership.
3. **30-Day Action:** LD reserve augmented from $12M to minimum $25M. CFO approval required.
4. **30-Day Action:** Formal EAC revision submitted incorporating shortage impacts under base-case and worst-case scenarios.
5. **30-Day Action:** Corporate CFO briefed on potential need for reserve support if mitigation costs exceed $50M.
6. **Ongoing:** All shortage-related expenditures tracked to dedicated WBS cost account for isolation and potential contract modification recovery.

**Near-Term:** Engage Collins Aerospace (primary integrator) to lead a consolidated supplier allocation negotiation on behalf of the program. Collins' contractual position may provide leverage at the foundry level that Eurus cannot achieve directly.

Qualifying a second source for IMA processors is a medium-term solution that does not resolve the immediate shortage but is essential for long-term program resilience. - ICD-003 compatibility verification: 1–3 months (parallel with above)
