## 8. Quality Management Plan

### 8.1 Quality Posture Under Shortage Conditions

The IMA core processor shortage creates specific quality risks that are distinct from those managed under steady-state program conditions. The Quality Manager is directed to issue updated surveillance and inspection guidance within 14 days covering the shortage-specific quality scenarios described in this section.

### 8.2 Supplier Quality Surveillance — Enhanced Requirements

The Quality Management Plan's requirements for supplier quality agreements and AS9100D compliance remain in full force and are not reduced by the shortage. The shortage, in fact, creates elevated quality pressure on the supplier: suppliers facing delivery commitments with constrained material sometimes introduce quality shortcuts including acceptance of marginal lots, relaxation of incoming inspection standards, or component substitution without formal engineering disposition.

The following enhanced surveillance requirements apply to all IMA processor deliveries during the shortage period:

- **100% Lot Acceptance Testing:** No sampling plan is acceptable for flight-critical avionics processors during the shortage period. Every unit must be individually tested against the qualification baseline before acceptance.
- **Full Traceability Verification:** Date code, lot number, and qualification test data must be verified for every delivered unit. Any unit without complete traceability documentation is rejected without exception.
- **Counterfeit Parts Screening:** All processor units procured through non-standard channels (broker markets, spot markets, alternative suppliers) must undergo counterfeit part detection screening per AS6081 and AS5553. The Security Requirements document identifies supply chain infiltration as a Medium-risk concern; counterfeit semiconductor insertion is a known threat vector for this program and is elevated to High during shortage conditions when broker procurement is active.
- **Process Change Notification:** Any change to the supplier's manufacturing process, sub-tier source, or component configuration requires formal supplier deviation/waiver processing through the program CCB before the modified article can be accepted for flight use. This is non-negotiable.

### 8.3 First Article Inspection Requirements

If the processor shortage forces the supplier to change their manufacturing process, sub-tier, or component configuration to fulfill delivery commitments, a new First Article Inspection (FAI) per AS9102 is required before the modified article can be accepted for flight use. The Quality Manager will establish a monitoring protocol to detect any supplier configuration changes that would trigger this requirement. The FAI requirement adds 4–12 weeks to the acceptance timeline and must be planned into the schedule recovery assessment.

### 8.4 Broker and Spot Market Procurement — Special Handling

If broker or spot market procurement is authorized under the buffer stock strategy (Section 7), the following quality controls apply and are mandatory. These controls are non-negotiable for DAL A flight-critical hardware:

1. Only authorized aerospace component distributors (Arrow, Avnet, TTI) with established anti-counterfeit programs may be used as primary broker sources.
2. Secondary brokers with 100% counterfeit screening capability and full chain-of-custody documentation may be approved on a case-by-case basis by the Quality Manager.
3. Every broker-sourced unit must pass 100% incoming inspection including electrical testing, X-ray inspection, decapsulation sampling, and date code verification before being admitted to the program parts inventory.
4. Broker-sourced units are quarantined from flight article installation until the full incoming inspection cycle is complete and documented.
5. Any broker-sourced unit that fails to provide complete traceability to the original semiconductor manufacturer is rejected and destroyed. There is no waiver path for this requirement.

### 8.5 Alternate Processor Qualification Quality Requirements

If an alternate processor is pursued under the Section 5.1 waiver path, the full DO-254 hardware qualification cycle applies without abbreviation. The Quality Manager and Certification Manager will jointly define the qualification test plan, which must include:
- Environmental qualification (temperature, humidity, vibration, shock, altitude) per the applicable avionics qualification standard (RTCA/DO-160)
- Reliability demonstration testing to validate KSA-01 MTBSA ≥ 25 flight hours threshold
- Electromagnetic compatibility (EMC) testing per applicable standards
- Software target environment compliance verification per DO-178C (this is the most time-intensive element, as all DAL A software running on the new processor must be re-verified in the new target environment)

A commercial-grade processor cannot be substituted for a DO-254-qualified flight-critical IMA processor without executing this full qualification cycle. There is no abbreviated path for DAL A hardware under any circumstance.

### 8.6 Out-of-Sequence Assembly Quality Considerations

If the build sequence reordering recommended in Section 3.3 results in partial aircraft builds (airframes and structural systems assembled without avionics), the following quality requirements apply:

- Open avionics bays on partially built aircraft are subject to a formal Foreign Object Damage (FOD) prevention program. Avionics bay covers must be installed and logged out-in-out at every access.
- All partial builds must be documented with an as-built configuration record that explicitly identifies the deferred avionics installation.
- Workmanship standards for avionics installation when processors eventually arrive must not be degraded by time pressure to close the schedule gap. The Quality Manager will issue explicit workmanship guidance before avionics installation resumes on any partial-build aircraft.

### 8.7 "Right First Time" Policy — Reaffirmation

The QMP's "Right First Time" policy is explicitly reaffirmed in the context of this shortage. Schedule pressure generated by the processor gap must not be used to justify quality holds being lifted prematurely, inspection requirements being waived, or rework being accepted without full disposition. Any quality escape during the shortage period that results in a rework loop will compound the schedule damage already caused by the supply disruption. The Quality Manager has standing authority to stop work at any point where quality requirements are being compromised by schedule pressure, and this authority is reinforced by this PMP Update.

## 9. Staffing & Resource Management

### 9.1 Workforce Impact Overview

The IMA core processor shortage does not reduce the program's workforce headcount requirement — the program will need every planned position when production resumes. However, it creates a significant workforce utilization challenge during the shortage window. Personnel hired or retained to support avionics integration, LRIP build-up, and flight test article completion will have reduced productive work scope during the period when IMA processor hardware is unavailable. Managing this workforce utilization gap is a critical leadership responsibility that directly affects both cost and learning curve continuity.

### 9.2 Avionics Integration and Systems Engineering Team

The avionics integration team supporting DTA-3 (WBS 4.6.3) and the AFCS IPT are the most directly affected workforce segments. Without physical IMA processor hardware, avionics bay integration and software integration activities on DTA-3 cannot proceed. The following workforce management actions are directed:

**Redeployment to Parallel Work:** The avionics integration workforce should be redirected during the processor gap to activities that do not require physical processor hardware:
- Avionics bay structural preparation and wiring harness installation (can proceed without processors)
- Test procedure development and validation on non-flight test bench equipment
- DO-178C software development and unit testing (processors are not required for early software development stages)
- HIL lab infrastructure preparation (racks, cabling, power, networking — everything except the flight-representative processors)
- Technical documentation, qualification test planning, and certification data package preparation

**FPGA Emulation for Early Software Integration:** The Chief Systems Engineer will assess within 21 days whether FPGA-based processor emulation boards can substitute for production-representative processors in the early software integration environment, enabling DO-178C qualification testing to begin ahead of hardware availability. This approach preserves software team productivity and may recover 2–4 months of schedule upon hardware availability.

**Workforce Retention Priority:** The avionics integration workforce represents specialized skills with long development times — DO-178C DAL A software engineers, avionics IPT systems engineers, and hardware qualification specialists are not fungible with general production labor. Retention of this workforce during the gap period is a higher priority than cost reduction through layoffs, because losing trained personnel now would extend the actual disruption beyond the semiconductor shortage window. Workforce retention incentives should be considered for key personnel with skill sets critical to post-shortage recovery.

### 9.3 Flight Test Team

The flight test team supporting FTA fabrication and the TEMP test program is similarly affected by the processor shortage. FTA avionics installation cannot be completed without processors; however, significant FTA build progress is achievable in parallel:
- Airframe fabrication and structural assembly (all FTAs)
- Propulsion system installation and testing
- Landing gear and hydraulic system integration
- Non-avionics ground systems installation
- Flight test instrumentation installation (instrumentation packages may use different processor hardware than the production avionics system)
- Ground support equipment preparation and calibration

The Manufacturing Director will develop a revised FTA build plan within 30 days that identifies the maximum production value-add achievable on each FTA without IMA processors, and sequences this work to maintain workforce productivity and preserve learning curve progress.

### 9.4 Production Manufacturing Team

The LRIP production manufacturing team is at risk of forced underutilization if LRIP start slips due to the shortage. The Manufacturing Ramp Plan's LRIP-2 phase gate (Q1 2027) requires MRL-8 achievement and supplier on-time delivery greater than 90% for 3 consecutive months. An avionics supplier in active shortage will fail this criterion, potentially blocking phase gate approval and preventing the planned production rate increase.

During the shortage window, the production manufacturing team should be directed to:
- Complete all non-avionics assembly preparation activities that can be performed without IMA processors
- Advance facility and tooling readiness activities for LRIP-2 and LRIP-3 rate increases
- Execute training and skills development for personnel who will perform avionics installation and systems integration once processors are available
- Support the alternate processor qualification effort with production tooling and fixture development

### 9.5 Supply Chain Organization Resource Requirements

The shortage significantly increases the workload on the Supply Chain organization. The following resource additions are authorized immediately:

- **Dedicated Shortage Response Team:** A cross-functional team of 4–6 personnel is established within the Supply Chain organization reporting directly to the Supply Chain Director. This team is responsible for daily monitoring of processor allocation status, supplier engagement, broker market surveillance, and buffer stock tracking. This team has no other responsibilities during the active shortage period.
- **Resident Quality Representative:** One Quality Engineer is assigned as resident representative at the flight computer supplier facility within 30 days. Travel and accommodation costs are charged to the shortage response cost account.
- **Legal/Contracts Support:** Additional Contracts and Legal resource is allocated for force majeure assessment, supplier LD notification, and potential contract modification negotiation. If internal capacity is insufficient, outside counsel authorization is requested from the General Counsel within 30 days.

### 9.6 Learning Curve Management — Critical Human Factors Dimension

The 85% learning curve assumption underpinning the FFP pricing is critically dependent on production continuity. A 12-month production stoppage does not merely pause learning — it actively degrades it. Personnel who performed avionics integration 12 months ago and then were redeployed to other activities will not return to their previous performance level immediately. The effective learning curve upon restart is likely to be 88%–90% rather than 85%, generating $40M–$60M in additional labor cost growth.

To mitigate learning curve regression:
- Workforce redeployment during the gap must, wherever possible, involve work in the same skill domain (avionics, systems integration) rather than unrelated activities
- Structured refresher training for avionics integration personnel will be planned and budgeted before production resumes
- The first LRIP aircraft processed through avionics integration after the shortage resolves should be treated as a de facto re-first-article from a quality and productivity monitoring standpoint, with enhanced supervision and first-unit review cycles

### 9.7 Key Personnel Dependency Management

The shortage concentrates risk on the few individuals with deep expertise in IMA processor architecture, DO-178C target environment management, and AFCS integration. These individuals will be under exceptional demand during the alternate processor assessment, MOSA compliance verification, and (if required) DO-254 re-qualification efforts. The Human Resources function will assess the key personnel dependency landscape within 30 days and recommend retention measures for individuals whose departure during the shortage window would materially impair recovery.

## 10. Overall Assessment & Recommended Path Forward

### 10.1 Executive Summary of Program Exposure

The 12-month IMA core processor semiconductor allocation shortage is the most significant single supply chain event the MAAP-1 program has encountered. It is not, at its core, a supply chain problem with a supply chain solution — it is a **program-level strategic event** that simultaneously threatens the financial baseline, the schedule baseline, the configuration baseline, the certification baseline, and the customer relationship. It demands a program-level response led by the Program Director with corporate visibility, not a supply chain corrective action plan managed below the VP level.

The exposure is quantified and bounded. The unmitigated cost impact of $85M–$118M would exhaust the $74M management reserve entirely and materially impair the 9.1% gross margin on a Firm Fixed Price contract with no recovery mechanism from the customer for commercial supply chain events. The schedule impact, if unmitigated, is a 12-month cascade from DTA-3 through First Flight, LRIP start, and IOC — a program-level milestone breach with contractual consequences including liquidated damages at $150K per week and potential FRP lot option reduction by the customer.

The good news is that the exposure is manageable if action is taken within the 72-hour window established by this PMP Update. The mitigation economics are unambiguous: spending $25M–$40M now avoids $85M–$118M in disruption costs. Every week of delay in executing the immediate actions described in this document represents additional schedule consumed and additional cost exposure crystallized.

### 10.2 Five Non-Negotiable Immediate Decisions

The following five decisions must be made within 72 hours of this document's approval. They are not recommendations — they are the minimum necessary actions to prevent the program from entering an unrecoverable posture. Each is assigned to a named decision authority.

**Decision 1 — Emergency Buffer Stock Authorization**
*Decision Authority: Program Director + CFO*
*Action:* Authorize emergency procurement of up to 30–40 IMA core processor units from all available sources, at a budget authority of up to $25M drawn from management reserve. Procurement to begin immediately upon authorization. Counterfeit screening (AS6081) mandatory for all non-standard source units.
*Consequence of inaction:* Every day without authorization is a day the buffer stock window narrows. Processor allocation availability in shortage conditions is first-come, first-served. Delay directly reduces the achievable buffer quantity.

**Decision 2 — Supplier Executive Escalation and Formal Recovery Plan Demand**
*Decision Authority: VP Supply Chain*
*Action:* Issue formal supplier notification to the flight computer supplier within 5 business days invoking contractual reporting obligations, demanding a written recovery plan within 15 business days, and formally documenting the notification date for potential force majeure and LD purposes.
*Consequence of inaction:* Without a formal demand, the supplier has no contractual urgency to provide a recovery plan. The program loses contractual leverage and the force majeure notification clock continues to run against Eurus Systems.

**Decision 3 — Customer Notification**
*Decision Authority: Program Manager*
*Action:* Issue formal customer notification within 5 business days per the event-driven communication protocol. Notification must cover: confirmed shortage scope, mitigation actions initiated, schedule risk range (not point estimate) for affected milestones, and decision points requiring customer input. Do not wait until the full picture is clear — the notification obligation runs from the date of discovery, not the date of certainty.
*Consequence of inaction:* Late customer notification destroys the trust relationship needed to negotiate schedule relief, LD waivers, and potential contract modifications. It also eliminates the force majeure notification window if one is applicable.

**Decision 4 — Emergency PCB Session**
*Decision Authority: Program Manager*
*Action:* Convene an emergency PCB session within 5 business days to formally register the shortage as a Class I event, approve the build sequence prioritization framework, authorize the management reserve drawdown, and assign ownership of all Tier 1 mitigation actions.
*Consequence of inaction:* Without formal PCB registration, mitigation actions will be executed outside the change control framework, creating configuration audit findings, untracked cost growth, and potential MDA visibility gaps.

**Decision 5 — Alternate Processor Trade Study Initiation**
*Decision Authority: Chief Engineer*
*Action:* Task the Avionics IPT to initiate a formal alternate processor trade study within 30 days, assessing: MOSA-1 compliance, ICD-003 compatibility, KSA-01 reliability implications, and DO-254/DO-178C re-qualification scope. The trade study does not commit the program to a substitution — it creates the informed basis for the Gate 3 decision at Day 60.
*Consequence of inaction:* If the trade study is not begun now, the Gate 3 decision at Day 60 will have no factual basis, and the program will be forced to either wait passively for the shortage to resolve or make an uninformed substitution decision. Neither is acceptable.

### 10.3 Decision Hierarchy and Trade-Space Adjudication

Multiple competing pressures will operate simultaneously during the shortage response: pressure to keep the production line moving, pressure to minimize cost premium for buffer stock, pressure to resolve the shortage quickly with a hardware substitution, and pressure to maintain the certification baseline. The following priority hierarchy, derived directly from Program Requirements Baseline Section 2.3, governs all trade-space adjudication:

**Priority 1 — Flight Safety and Airworthiness:** No action that compromises the autonomy safety case, the DO-178C/DO-254 qualification baseline, or the FAA certification data package is acceptable regardless of schedule or cost pressure. A non-qualified processor is not an acceptable bridge solution. A qualification shortcut is not acceptable regardless of supplier assurances.

**Priority 2 — Mission Performance KPPs:** KPP-06 (Net-Ready) and KPP-07 (Autonomous Operation) are JROC-controlled requirements. Any hardware substitution that cannot be demonstrated to maintain these KPPs at or above their threshold values is not acceptable without MDA concurrence.

**Priority 3 — Schedule:** Schedule recovery is a legitimate program objective, but it is explicitly subordinate to Safety and KPP maintenance. Schedule pressure must not drive quality, safety, or certification compromises.

**Priority 4 — Cost:** Cost efficiency is important on a margin-stressed FFP program, but the hierarchy makes clear that spending appropriately on buffer stock, alternate qualification, and supplier escalation is the correct decision even if it is expensive. The alternative — a non-airworthy aircraft or a missed certification milestone — is far more costly.

### 10.4 Strategic Path Forward

The program's response to this shortage will define its resilience posture for the remainder of the production run. The following strategic path is recommended:

**Phase 1 (Days 0–30): Stabilize**
Protect the development and early flight test build schedule through emergency buffer stock procurement. Prevent the shortage from consuming the DTA-3 and FTA-1/2 schedule. Establish formal governance through PCB Class I process. Notify the customer and preserve force majeure rights. Do not re-baseline the IMS. Do not make premature substitution decisions.

**Phase 2 (Days 30–90): Assess and Decide**
Receive and validate the supplier's recovery plan. Complete the alternate processor trade study. Make the Gate 3 decision: wait for primary processor recovery with buffer stock protection, or pursue a qualified alternate. If alternate is pursued, initiate the Section 5.1 waiver process and DO-254/DO-178C qualification path immediately. Conduct the IMS CPM re-analysis and prepare revised milestone dates for customer negotiation. Assess force majeure applicability and initiate contract modification discussions.

**Phase 3 (Days 90–365): Recover and Harden**
Execute the chosen mitigation path — buffer stock protection or alternate processor qualification — with full governance discipline. Negotiate customer concurrence on revised milestone dates. Draw down management reserve in a controlled and documented manner. Accelerate the dual-source qualification effort to LRIP-1 completion. Negotiate long-term foundry allocation agreements covering FRP quantities. Revise the supply chain resilience framework to address market-wide semiconductor allocation shortages — a risk category that was not previously modeled in the program's risk management architecture.

**Phase 4 (Post-Recovery): Prevent Recurrence**
Upon shortage resolution, conduct a formal lessons-learned review. Revise the Program Requirements Baseline to include a Supply Chain Risk Management Plan annex establishing LLI registry maintenance, minimum buffer stock calculation methodology, and sole-source risk mitigation requirements for all common Green Aircraft configuration items. Update the Vertical Integration and Make/Buy Strategy to increase buffer stock design basis from 6 months to 12–18 months for semiconductor-dependent avionics components. Institutionalize Tier 2/3 supply chain visibility for all flight-critical processor components. These changes must be in place before FRP lot planning begins.

### 10.5 The One Overriding Principle

Every decision made during this shortage response must be traceable to a single principle: **the program must deliver airworthy aircraft that meet their KPPs on a schedule that can be defended to the customer, the regulator, and the Milestone Decision Authority.** No shortcut that compromises airworthiness is recoverable. No informal decision that bypasses the PCB and CCB process is auditable. No buffer stock procurement that skips counterfeit screening is acceptable for DAL A hardware. The shortage is a serious but survivable event. A compromised aircraft or a failed certification audit is not.

Act now. Act in order of priority. Act within the governance framework. The program has the tools, the reserves, and the architectural flexibility — through the MOSA open architecture, the multi-vendor OpenVPX ecosystem, and the documented dual-source qualification path — to emerge from this shortage with its technical baseline and customer relationships intact. The window for decisive action is open today. It will not remain open indefinitely.
